title: Athyrium epirachis
taxonomy: Athyrium epirachis is a species of fern in the family Athyriaceae. It is native to New Guinea. Within the kingdom Plantae, Athyrium epirachis is classified as a vascular plant and belongs to the division Pteridophyta. This species of fern is further classified as a member of the order Polypodiales and the genus Athyrium. The classification of Athyrium epirachis reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary relationships within the plant kingdom.
uses: Athyrium epirachis, a species of fern, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves are known to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in reducing pain and inflammation. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and constipation, due to its astringent properties. Additionally, the plant's extracts have been studied for their potential to inhibit the growth of certain microorganisms, making it a possible candidate for wound healing and antimicrobial applications. Further research is necessary to confirm the full extent of its medicinal properties.

intro: Athyrium epirachis, also known as Japanese lady fern, is a species of fern native to Japan, characterized by its delicate and lacy fronds. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may help to alleviate conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Athyrium epirachis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Athyrium epirachis is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used to make tea, tinctures, and other herbal remedies.
